summary: Luu argues that engineering education systematically fails students by refusing to teach fundamental debugging skills, instead treating high failure rates as a badge of honor that weeds out those who "can't hack it." He recounts discovering systematic debugging through necessity during a divider project, realizing that many struggling students weren't lacking intelligence but simply had never been taught methodical problem-solving approaches. The education system's pride in its "leaky STEM pipeline" is a manufactured crisis—schools could easily teach these skills but choose not to, preferring hazing rituals over actual education.
key_points: Many engineering students fail not due to lack of ability, but because fundamental skills like systematic debugging are never taught
The "Feynman Method" (think really hard) works for simple problems but fails on complex real-world projects without systematic debugging techniques
Educators often rationalize high failure rates as evidence that "some students just can't hack it" rather than acknowledging instructional failures
A simple half-hour lesson on systematic debugging could save a significant fraction of students who wash out and switch majors
The more talented the engineer, the more likely they are to hit a debugging wall outside of school if they've never learned systematic approaches
money_quotes: Why do we leave material out of classes and then fail students who can't figure out that material for themselves?
The more talented the engineer, the more likely they are to hit a debugging wall outside of school.
For all the high-level talk about how we need to plug the leaks in our STEM education pipeline, not only are we not plugging the holes, we're proud of how fast the pipeline is leaking.
I was told in no uncertain terms that it would be a waste of time because some people just can't hack it in engineering.
It should be possible to systematically apply a mechanical technique repeatedly to find the source of our problems.
key_insight: Engineering education's high failure rates are a manufactured crisis resulting from deliberate choices not to teach fundamental debugging skills, not evidence that struggling students lack aptitude.
